title: Fixing GIT Ref Name Validation for Non-ASCII Characters

statement.markdown: For this task, you will implement a function that takes a string as input and validates if it is a valid GIT ref name. A valid GIT ref name must match the following pattern: `[a-zA-Z0-9_.-]+`. However, this pattern does not account for non-ASCII characters. You need to extend the pattern to support non-ASCII characters. The function should return `true` if the input string is a valid GIT ref name, and `false` otherwise.
statement.notes: Note that GIT ref names are case-sensitive. You should also handle edge cases such as empty strings, null inputs, and non-string inputs.

examples.explanation: Valid GIT ref name (empty string is a valid ref name)

hints.text: Use a regular expression to match the pattern
Consider using Unicode character classes to support non-ASCII characters

editorial.title: Editorial Solution
editorial.intuition: The key idea is to extend the pattern to support non-ASCII characters using Unicode character classes
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach involves checking each character individually, which is inefficient
editorial.betterSolution: Regular expressions provide a concise and efficient way to match patterns
editorial.optimalSolution: Use the `^` and `$` anchors to ensure the entire string matches the pattern
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The solution correctly handles edge cases and supports non-ASCII characters
